title: Writing - Part 1: Urban Green Spaces
description: Your class has attended a panel discussion on the role of parks and green spaces in cities and whether urban planners are investing enough in them.

content.question: Write an essay discussing two of the benefits in your notes. You should explain which benefit you consider most important for city residents, giving reasons for your opinion.

opinions: Green spaces in cities are directly linked to better mental health.
Urban trees reduce pollution, manage flooding, and cool the city.
Parks are rare spaces where people from all backgrounds mix.

structure.introduction: Introduce the importance of green spaces in modern urban environments.
structure.bodyParagraph1: Examine the well-documented link between green spaces and mental health outcomes.
structure.bodyParagraph2: Discuss the environmental benefits that urban vegetation provides.
structure.conclusion: Evaluate both benefits and argue which is most significant for the well-being of city populations.

content.modelAnswer:
As cities grow denser and the pace of modern life more relentless, the value of urban green spaces—parks, gardens, and tree-lined streets—has never been more evident. This essay will examine two of the principal benefits they provide: their contribution to mental health and their role as environmental infrastructure.

The evidence linking access to green space with psychological well-being is now substantial. Studies consistently demonstrate that residents with regular access to parks and natural environments report lower levels of stress, anxiety, and depression than those living in entirely built-up areas. The mechanisms are both physiological and psychological: exposure to natural environments reduces cortisol levels, encourages physical activity, and provides relief from the cognitive overload that characterises urban life. For cities grappling with an epidemic of mental health problems, investing in accessible green space may represent one of the most cost-effective public health interventions available.

Equally important, however, is the environmental function that urban vegetation performs. Trees absorb carbon dioxide, filter air pollutants, and provide cooling through evapotranspiration—an effect that becomes increasingly critical as urban heat islands intensify with climate change. Green spaces also manage surface water, reducing the risk of flooding during extreme rainfall events. These services are not amenities but infrastructure, as essential to a functioning city as roads and sewers.

In conclusion, while both benefits are genuinely significant, I consider the mental health case the more compelling priority for urban planners. As populations become increasingly urbanised, ensuring that all residents have meaningful access to nature may be among the most important contributions a city can make to human flourishing.
